analogy: Think of "Transport in Plants" through the chapter's own opening images: UNIT 4 The description of structure and variation of living organisms over a period of time, ended up as two, apparently irreconcilable perspectives on biology. The two perspectives essentially rested on two levels of organisation of life forms and phenomena. One described at organismic and... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Transport in Plants is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that uNIT 4 The description of structure and variation of living organisms over a period of time, ended up as two, apparently irreconcilable perspectives on biology.

The text explains that the two perspectives essentially rested on two levels of organisation of life forms and phenomena.

The text explains that one described at organismic and above level of organisation while the second described at cellular and molecular level of organisation.

The text explains that the first resulted in ecology and related disciplines.

The text explains that the second resulted in physiology and biochemistry.

The text explains that description of physiological processes, in flowering plants as an example, is what is given in the chapters in this unit.

The text explains that the processes of photosynthesis, respiration and ultimately plant growth and development are described in molecular terms but in the context of cellular activities and even at organism level.

The text explains that wherever appropriate, the relation of the physiological processes to environment is also discussed.

The text explains that pLANT PHYSIOLOGY Chapter 11 Photosynthesis in Higher Plants Chapter 12 Respiration in Plants Chapter 13 Plant Growth and Development

The text explains that mELVIN CALVIN born in Minnesota in April, 1911, received his Ph.D. in Chemistry from the University of Minnesota.

The text explains that he served as Professor of Chemistry at the University of California, Berkeley.

The text explains that just after world war II, when the world was under shock after the Hiroshima-Nagasaki bombings, and seeing the ill- effects of radio-activity, Calvin and co-workers put radio- activity to beneficial use.

The text explains that bassham studied reactions in green plants forming sugar and other substances from raw materials like carbon dioxide, water and minerals by labelling the carbon dioxide with C14.

The text explains that calvin proposed that plants change light energy to chemical energy by transferring an electron in an organised array of pigment molecules and other substances.

The text explains that the mapping of the pathway of carbon assimilation in photosynthesis earned him Nobel Prize in 1961.

The text explains that the principles of photosynthesis as established by Calvin are, at present, being used in studies on renewable resource for energy and materials and basic studies in solar energy research.

The text explains that photosynthesis is important due to two reasons: it is the primary source of all food on earth.

The text explains that the former set of reactions, since they are directly light driven are called light reactions (photochemical reactions).

The text explains that the latter are not directly light driven but are dependent on the products of light reactions (ATP and NADPH).

The text explains that pigments are substances that have an ability to absorb light, at specific wavelengths.

The text explains that let us study the graph showing the ability of chlorophyll a pigment to absorb lights of different wavelengths (Figure 11.3 a).

The text explains that of course, you are familiar with the wavelength of the visible spectrum of light as well as the VIBGYOR.

The text explains that from Figure 11.3a can you determine the wavelength (colour of light) at which chlorophyll a shows the maximum absorption?

The text explains that does it show another absorption peak at any other wavelengths too?

The text explains that now look at Figure 11.3b showing the wavelengths at which maximum photosynthesis occurs in a plant.

The text explains that can you see that the wavelengths at which there is maximum absorption by chlorophyll a, i.e., in the blue and the red regions, also shows higher rate of photosynthesis.

The text explains that 138 BIOLOGY These graphs, together, show that most of the photosynthesis takes place in the blue and red regions of the spectrum; some photosynthesis does take place at the other wavelengths of the visible spectrum.

The text explains that indeed, they not only enable a wider range of wavelength of incoming light to be utilised for photosyntesis but also protect chlorophyll a from photo-oxidation.

The text explains that these pigments help to make photosynthesis more efficient by absorbing different wavelengths of light.

The text explains that 11.6 THE ELECTRON TRANSPORT In photosystem II the reaction centre chlorophyll a absorbs 680 nm wavelength of red light causing electrons to become excited and jump into an orbit farther from the atomic nucleus.

The text explains that simultaneously, electrons in the reaction centre of PS I are also excited when they receive red light of wavelength 700 nm and are transferred to another accepter molecule that has a greater redox potential.

The text explains that cyclic photophosphorylation also occurs when only light of wavelengths beyond 680 nm are available for excitation.

The text explains that this process does not directly depend on the presence of light but is dependent on the products of the light reaction, i.e., ATP and NADPH, besides CO2 and H2O.

The text explains that c4 plants are special: They have a special type of leaf anatomy, they tolerate higher temperatures, they show a response to high light intensities, they lack a process called photorespiration and have greater productivity of biomass.

The text explains that in addition, these plants show tolerance to higher temperatures.

The text explains that cO2 fixation rate under high Low/ high/ medium light conditions Whether photorespiration is High/negligible/sometimes present at low light intensities Whether photorespiration is High/negligible/sometimes present at high light intensities Whether photorespiration would be High/negligible/sometimes present at low CO2 concentrations Whether photorespiration would be High/negligible/sometimes present at high CO2 concentrations Temperature optimum 30-40°C/20-25°C/above 40°C Examples Cut vertical sections of leaves of different plants and observe under the microscope for Kranz anatomy and list them in the appropriate columns.

The text explains that the external factors would include the availability of sunlight, temperature, CO2 concentration and water.

The text explains that this states the following: If a chemical process is affected by more than one factor, then its rate will be determined by the factor which is nearest to its minimal value: it is the factor which directly affects the process if its quantity is changed.

The text explains that for example, despite the presence of a green leaf and optimal light and CO2 conditions, the plant may not photosynthesise if the temperature is very low.

The text explains that this leaf, if given the optimal temperature, will start photosynthesising.

The text explains that 11.10.3 Temperature The dark reactions being enzymatic are temperature controlled.

The text explains that though the light reactions are also temperature sensitive they are affected to a much lesser extent.

The text explains that the C4 plants respond to higher temperatures and show higher rate of photosynthesis while C3 plants have a much lower temperature optimum.

The text explains that the temperature optimum for photosynthesis of different plants also depends on the habitat that they are adapted to.

The text explains that tropical plants have a higher temperature optimum than the plants adapted to temperate climates.

The text explains that 11.10.4 Water Even though water is one of the reactants in the light reaction, the effect of water as a factor is more through its effect on the plant, rather than directly on photosynthesis.

The text explains that why do you think RuBisCO carries out more carboxylation in C4 plants?

The text explains that suppose there were plants that had a high concentration of Chlorophyll b, but lacked chlorophyll a, would it carry out photosynthesis?

The text explains that then why do plants have chlorophyll b and other accessory pigments?

The text explains that why is the colour of a leaf kept in the dark frequently becomes yellow, or pale green?

The text explains that look at leaves of the same plant on the shady side and compare it with the leaves on the sunny side.

The text explains that or, compare the potted plants kept in the sunlight with those in the shade.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Biology, Chapter 11: Transport in Plants

prelims_traps: Do not treat PLANT PHYSIOLOGY and PHOTOSYNTHESIS IN HIGHER PLANTS 133 as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Transport in Plants; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Transport in Plants through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use PLANT PHYSIOLOGY and PHOTOSYNTHESIS IN HIGHER PLANTS 133 to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
